title: Fitting an electric underfloor heating mat under tiles
subtitle: Install a thin electric heating mat beneath a tiled floor
excerpt: How to fit a self-adhesive electric underfloor heating mat under ceramic or porcelain tiles, covering the planning and layout, embedding the mat in adhesive, running the cable to a thermostat, and commissioning the circuit. Covers standard floor tile adhesive over an electric mat.
sourceNotes: IET Wiring Regulations (BS 7671), Part P Building Regulations (England and Wales). Electric Underfloor Heating Association installation guidance (open-access). BS 8204 screed standards. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

glossaryTerms.definition: A thin mesh mat with a heating element cable woven through it in a serpentine pattern. Typically 3 mm thick. Rolled out onto the floor, embedded in tile adhesive, and connected to a thermostat and the ring circuit. Provides radiant warmth to the tile surface above. The element is rated in watts per square metre; bathroom mats are typically 150 W/m².

A wall-mounted thermostat that controls the temperature of an electric underfloor heating system. A floor-sensing probe (embedded in the screed or adhesive) measures the floor temperature; the thermostat turns the element on and off to maintain the set temperature. Programmed to run before the room is occupied for best efficiency.

content.text: adds only 3 mm to floor build-up and is laid directly under the tile adhesive bed. The connection from the mat to the

counts as fixed electrical work under Part P of the Building Regulations in England and Wales; the connection must either be made by a registered competent person or notified to Building Control. The tiling work above it is straightforward once the mat is embedded. Never cut the heating element cable; cutting the mesh carrier is fine when routing around obstacles.
Step 0, measure the area and check the circuit

Measure the floor area to be heated; exclude the space under fixed furniture (kitchen units, bath, WC). Order a mat sized to cover the open floor area only. Mats come in fixed sizes (0.5 m², 1 m², 2 m², etc.) and the element spacing is fixed, you cannot overlap mats or stretch a single mat to cover a larger area. Verify that the circuit feeding the thermostat outlet has enough spare capacity for the mat's wattage load. A 150 W/m² mat over 3 m² is 450 W; a 13 A socket circuit handles this comfortably alongside normal room loads.

The subfloor must be clean, level, and free of loose material. Any high spots more than 3 mm in 2 m should be ground down or filled with floor levelling compound before the mat goes down. Vacuum thoroughly. On a timber subfloor, add a layer of 6 mm fibre cement board screwed at 200 mm intervals to give a rigid, stable base; this prevents differential flex between the heated and unheated areas cracking the grout.
Step 2, plan the mat layout and chase the wall

Unroll the mat dry first and plan how it fits the room. The mat can be bent back on itself (routing the mesh, not the element) to fill awkward shapes. Mark the route with masking tape. Chase a 20 mm channel in the wall from the thermostat position down to floor level using the cordless drill and a suitable bit; this is where the cold-tail cable from the mat will run. Feed the cold-tail cable up the chase to the thermostat position. Tape the mat to the subfloor with the self-adhesive mesh edge strips, keeping the element off the skirting boards by 50 mm on each side.
Step 3, test the mat before tiling

Check the mat's resistance with the multimeter set to ohms before any adhesive goes down. The resistance should be within 5% of the value on the mat's label (typically printed on a foil sticker supplied with the mat; keep this for the Building Control certificate). If the reading is outside this range, the element is damaged and the mat must be replaced before tiling. Record the resistance value; you will check it again after tiling to confirm no damage occurred during the tiling process.
Step 4, embed the mat in adhesive and tile over it

Apply a thin screed of flexible tile adhesive (typically 3 to 5 mm) over the entire mat, filling all the gaps between the element runs until the mat is fully submerged. Use a float to smooth the adhesive level; no notched trowel on the first pass as this can displace the element. Allow this bed coat to partially firm up (approximately 30 minutes), then apply the notched adhesive coat for the tiles in the normal way and set the tiles. The mat adds an insulating layer and the first tile laid over it may feel slightly stiffer to press; apply firm, even pressure to each tile.

Once the adhesive has cured (at least 24 hours, longer in cold conditions), check the mat resistance again with the multimeter. The reading must match the pre-tiling value within 5%; any significant change indicates element damage during tiling. Have the cold-tail connected to the thermostat by a registered electrician or make the connection yourself and notify Building Control. Do not run the heating until the adhesive and grout are fully cured: typically 7 days minimum. Start at the lowest thermostat setting and raise it by 5°C per day over the first week.

The heating element is set permanently into the adhesive and cannot be retrieved without breaking up the floor. Keep the installation certificate with the resistance reading somewhere safe. Run the floor on a timer to bring it up to temperature before use. Do not leave it running continuously at the maximum setting.

attrs.symptom: Mat resistance reading is significantly higher after tiling than before
attrs.cause: The element cable has been nicked or damaged by the trowel or by a tile edge during installation.
attrs.fix: Isolate the circuit. The element must be traced and repaired; this typically means removing tiles above the suspect area, splicing the element with a waterproof crimp splice kit designed for heating cable repair, and re-tiling. Do not commission a circuit with a significantly changed resistance.

attrs.symptom: The floor heats unevenly — some areas warm, others cold
attrs.cause: Mat overlap, or the element spacing was compressed during installation, concentrating heat in one area.
attrs.fix: Check that the mat mesh was laid flat without folding or overlapping the element. If the unheated area is only around fixed furniture, this is normal; the mat should not have covered that zone.

attrs.symptom: Grout cracks appear above the mat area within the first few months
attrs.cause: The mat was commissioned before the adhesive fully cured, causing differential expansion that cracked the grout joints.
attrs.fix: Allow 7 days minimum after tiling before switching on the system. Rake out cracked grout joints, re-grout, and allow to cure before running the heating again.
